EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On this day, a surface trough located near southeast New Mexico was the focus for some thunderstorm development.  The deep layer shear and surface based instability resulted in a couple of severe thunderstorms.

On this day, a surface trough located near southeast New Mexico was the focus for some thunderstorm development.  The deep layer shear and surface based instability resulted in a couple of severe thunderstorms.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system tracked from the Four Corners area east-southeast across New Mexico during the weekend of the 14th and 15th.   As it exited the area, a strong back door cold front surged south across the eastern plains.  Heaviest snow amounts were generally above 8000 feet across north central and northwest New Mexico.  However, modest accumulations were also noted across the northeast behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a foot of snow fell at the highest elevations, including at the Angel Fire Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system brought snow to much of New Mexico over a four day period.  Snow began over the northern mountains on the 28th and lingered through the morning hours of the 30th.  Amounts were typically light - on the order of 4 inches or less.   Then, as the system crossed northern old Mexico, significant snows developed over the south central mountains and adjacent plains to the east on the 30th through December 1st.  More than one foot of snow was reported across several of these locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 4 inches of snow was reported in and north of Tres Piedras.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system brought snow to much of New Mexico over a four day period.  Snow began over the northern mountains on the 28th and lingered through the morning hours of the 30th.  Amounts were typically light - on the order of 4 inches or less.   Then, as the system crossed northern old Mexico, significant snows developed over the south central mountains and adjacent plains to the east on the 30th through December 1st.  More than one foot of snow was reported across several of these locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snow accumulations blanketed eastern Lincoln County.  The heaviest amounts were across southeast Lincoln County where up to 9 inches of snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the deep layer shear this day was relatively weak compared to the previous days, there was plenty of boundary layer moisture/instability to work with. Steep mid-level lapse rates and low wet bulb zero heights also resulted in increased hail threats from any storms that developed within a theta-e ridge axis positioned over the east central CWA.

An upper level trough across the Great Basin resulted in southwest flow aloft across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico. The combination of disturbances moving northeast across the area, strong daytime heating and good low level moisture ahead of the dryline resulted in severe weather across the area. The main threat during this episode was large hail and strong damaging winds.

An upper level trough across the Great Basin resulted in southwest flow aloft across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico. The combination of disturbances moving northeast across the area, strong daytime heating and good low level moisture ahead of the dryline resulted in severe weather across the area. The main threat during this episode was large hail and strong damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous damage reports associated with this severe thunderstorm.  The damage path has been estimated according to these reports.  At 1945 MST, a trained spotter reported that a small camper was blown over onto a pickup truck near the intersection of Kincaid Rd and Highway 385.  Significant damage was done to the camper.  About five minutes later, 30' long by 20' wide metal sheds were destroyed about 5 miles south of Artesia and an Atoka, NM resident had his porch awning blown off his house.  Around 1955 MST, thunderstorm wind gusts snapped a power pole in half near the junction of 4 Dinkus Rd and Highway 285.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system tracked from the Four Corners area east-southeast across New Mexico during the weekend of the 14th and 15th.   As it exited the area, a strong back door cold front surged south across the eastern plains.  Heaviest snow amounts were generally above 8000 feet across north central and northwest New Mexico.  However, modest accumulations were also noted across the northeast behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were common across the northwest highlands. The greatest snow totals were found just southwest of Cuba.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system tracked from the Four Corners area east-southeast across New Mexico during the weekend of the 14th and 15th.   As it exited the area, a strong back door cold front surged south across the eastern plains.  Heaviest snow amounts were generally above 8000 feet across north central and northwest New Mexico.  However, modest accumulations were also noted across the northeast behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevations generally above 10000 feet received around 8 inches of snow, including the Santa Fe Ski area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active severe weather pattern set up over portions of southeast New Mexico on this day, with an upper level trough crossing the Rocky Mountains.  The large scale lift associated with this trough combined with the moisture available resulted in thunderstorm development across portions of Lea County New Mexico.

A strong upper level trough moved over the southern Rocky Mountains and southern U.S. Plains.  Very strong mid level winds at the base of this upper trough mixed down to the surface and resulted in strong winds across the mountains and plains of southeast New Mexico and west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts of 64 MPH were recorded around Noon LST at Tatum and Lovington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved over southeast New Mexico and west Texas late on 12/28/09 and early on 12/29/09.  Cold air over the region combined with this upper storm system to produce a variety of winter weather across southeast New Mexico and west Texas, in addition to snow over the Guadalupe Mountains and southeast New Mexico plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow accumulated in Tatum.  Roads were snow packed and icy.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow accumulated in Queen, most of which occurred between 0700 and 0900 LST with a burst of moderate to heavy snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm along the California coast brought strong winds to the Central High Plains on the 3rd, while snow fell over much of the west and north on the 4th into the 5th.
A slow moving storm along the California coast brought strong winds to the Central High Plains on the 3rd, while snow fell over much of the west and north on the 4th into the 5th.
Five mornings of temperatures in the single digits to the teens resulted in one death due to exposure on New Years Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 42 year old male was found dead in his truck, apparently as a result of continued exposure to the cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level jet max associated with a mid to upper level trough combined with a deep surface low resulted in high winds across a small portion of the plains of southeast New Mexico.

A strong mid level jet max rotated through the base of a strong upper level trough moving over the Northern Rockies.  These strong winds mixed towards the surface of the higher terrain of the Guadalupe Mountains of south central New Mexico and a portion of the adjacent plains of southeast New Mexico.

A storm from the Pacific Northwest slowly advanced toward New Mexico on the 25th, reached the Four Corners Region late on the 26th and inched across the state on the 27th. A strong cold front also backed down the eastern plains with strong and gusty winds. Moderate to heavy snow was reported over much of the northern half of the state, with blowing and|drifting snow and poor visibilities over the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between two and seven inches of snow fell over mainly the eastern and northern portions of the zone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Gallup woman, Denise Caleb, was killed in her SUV on Interstate 40 due to icy roads and windy conditions. Her vehicle was heading east on I-40 near Gallup when she lost control and crossed the median and went into oncoming traffic. Her SUV was struck by a tractor-trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Illinois man died when two vehicles crashed on Interstate 40, 12 miles west of Gallup. The freeway was coated with slush and ice from an overnight light snow event. A full sized van lost control on eastbound I-40 at mile marker 9, crossed the median and crashed into a SUV that was heading west at 800 am MDT. The van completely split the SUV in half. The driver of the SUV, 46 year old James Gasser, died instantly.

Extensive cloud cover during the day, mostly east of the Pecos River, only resulted in a couple of isolated severe storms over southeast NM for this event.  A mid to upper level shortwave trough and surface convergence a long a lee trough provided the lift during the evening hours.  Moderate surface based instability, 0 to 6 km bulk shear magnitudes near 50 kt and enhanced low level shear due to backing winds across the area resulted in a couple of weak tornadoes being produced by a long lived supercell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid level flow combined with a weak disturbance and associated jet max to produce high winds over portions of central and eastern New Mexico. High sustained winds and wind gusts were reported over a six hour period, between roughly 1100 am and 700 pm MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts between 59 mph and 61 mph were also recorded at the Las Vegas ASOS between 249 pm and 535 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough moved southeastward into northern New Mexico as a surface low deepened in the Texas panhandle. Strong pressure gradients at the surface and aloft, along with fast flow aloft mixing down to the surface resulted in strong gusty winds.

A strong upper trough moved across portions of Arizona into the region.  Accompanying this trough was a 120 knot upper level jet with 40 knot winds near the low levels of the atmosphere.  As a result, a deep surface low developed over southeastern Colorado and the Texas Panhandle resulting in a very tight pressure gradient and strong surface winds.  The winds also resulted in restricted visibilities of less than 1/4 mile for some locations.

A slow moving upper level low inched eastward across northern New Mexico between the 16th and the 18th of April. By early morning on the 17th, portions of the Northern Mountains and Northeast Highlands had already received a couple inches of snow. Snow spread southward across the Sandia Mountains during the afternoon and quickly dropped several inches of snow. Occasional rounds of thundersnow during the period resulted in locally higher amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snows of 3 to 7 inches were reported across the Sangre De Cristo Mountains.  However, thundersnow resulted in localized heavy snowfall near Gascon, where a trained spotter reported 13 inches. In addition, 8 inches of snow was reported northwest of Taos by a CoCoRaHS observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low inched eastward across northern New Mexico between the 16th and the 18th of April. By early morning on the 17th, portions of the Northern Mountains and Northeast Highlands had already received a couple inches of snow. Snow spread southward across the Sandia Mountains during the afternoon and quickly dropped several inches of snow. Occasional rounds of thundersnow during the period resulted in locally higher amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heaviest snow fell during the afternoon and evening hours of the 17th and it piled up quickly. Between 4 and 7 inches of snowfall was reported across the Sandia and Manzano Mountains.  Some totals include 7 inches 10 miles south of Tijeras, 5.5 inches 6 miles northeast of Cedar Crest and 4.5 inches 5 miles east-southeast of Sandia Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low inched eastward across northern New Mexico between the 16th and the 18th of April. By early morning on the 17th, portions of the Northern Mountains and Northeast Highlands had already received a couple inches of snow. Snow spread southward across the Sandia Mountains during the afternoon and quickly dropped several inches of snow. Occasional rounds of thundersnow during the period resulted in locally higher amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported 9.5 inches near El Porvenir, or about 8 miles northwest of Las Vegas. Thundersnow attributed to the high snowfall amounts in the northern appendage of the Central High Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season upper low moved slowly northeastward across Arizona and New Mexico, drawing up plenty of moisture in advance within a deep southerly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in some homes in the community of Rodey. There was also flooding along Highway 185 southeast of Hatch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with a cold front crossing New Mexico caused wind damage in Algodones to a mobile home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shingles were blown off a roof of a mobile home in Algodones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere had dried out somewhat in the wake of an upper trough, but enough mid level moisture remained for the formation of high based thunderstorms and dry microbursts.
A storm over the Great Basin provided a strong southwest flow, with isolated high winds around Gallup in the mid to late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts between 50 and 52 knots were recorded between 216pm and 459pm MST. A sustained wind speed of 37 knots was also recorded between 255pm and 355pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low inched eastward across northern New Mexico between the 16th and the 18th of April. By early morning on the 17th, portions of the Northern Mountains and Northeast Highlands had already received a couple inches of snow. Snow spread southward across the Sandia Mountains during the afternoon and quickly dropped several inches of snow. Occasional rounds of thundersnow during the period resulted in locally higher amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Though many locations across the Northwest Mountains above 7500 feet only received between 3 and 7 inches of snow, similar amounts were also observed below 7500 feet.  Spotters in Los Alamos estimate around 5 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough moved over the southern Rocky Mountains and southern U.S. Plains.  Very strong mid level winds at the base of this upper trough mixed down to the surface and resulted in strong winds across the mountains and plains of southeast New Mexico and west Texas.

A surface trough/convergence boundary draped across southeast New Mexico served as a focus for isolated severe convection on this day.  Deep layer shear was enhanced during the afternoon hours as the subtropical flow aloft increased.  In addition, MLCAPE values neared 1000 j/kg.  These two parameters contributed to small hail reports from Eddy County New Mexico during the late afternoon hours.

Deep layer shear was enhanced during the afternoon hours as the subtropical flow aloft increased.  In addition, MLCAPE values neared 1000 j/kg.  These two parameters contributed to small hail reports from Eddy County New Mexico during the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground about 4 inches in depth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary storm over Arizona provided an extended period of deep moisture with widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms. A few of the heavier showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rain. One such storm impacted the Northwest Plateau, resulting in localized flash flooding along state highway 491 south of Shiprock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused about four feet of mud to slide into an irrigation canal running along Navajo route 362. This forced water over route 362 and onto farm land and residents' homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front located near the Pecos River and surface based instability along the front resulted in thunderstorm development across southwest Texas.  In addition, abnormally high amounts of atmospheric moisture lead to heavy rainfall for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The post office in Loco Hills, NM estimated winds to be near 70 mph or greater, although no damage was officially reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front located near the Pecos River and surface based instability along the front resulted in thunderstorm development across southwest Texas.  In addition, abnormally high amounts of atmospheric moisture lead to heavy rainfall for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported numerous tree limbs, up to 4 inches in diamter, were blow down in Artesia, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over Arizona resulting in northerly flow aloft over southern New Mexico. In addition, a weak back door cold front moved southwestward across the area and stalled near the international border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel and quarter size hail fell near the cafe in Weed, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over Arizona resulting in northerly flow aloft over southern New Mexico. In addition, a weak back door cold front moved southwestward across the area and stalled near the international border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel and quarter size hail fell near the junction of US 82 and NM 130 in the vicinity of Mayhill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over Arizona resulting in northerly flow aloft over southern New Mexico. In addition, a weak back door cold front moved southwestward across the area and stalled near the international border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power outage resulted from 4 power poles blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak back door cold front pushed westward across southern New Mexico while a minor upper disturbance moved in from the west. These features had plenty of low level moisture to work with.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down 9 power poles about 6 miles west of Columbus, NM along NM Highway 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southerly flow was in place over the area. The atmospheric moisture content was almost twice the normal value for early July. The flow was such that thunderstorm movement was slow, subjecting locations to very heavy rainfall for long periods of time. Occasional wet microbursts were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of a bridge was damaged on US Highway 54 near mile marker 93. Edges of the roadway eroded and were washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southerly flow was in place over the area. The atmospheric moisture content was almost twice the normal value for early July. The flow was such that thunderstorm movement was slow, subjecting locations to very heavy rainfall for long periods of time. Occasional wet microbursts were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds blew part of a roof off of a Baptist church in Boles Acres. A gust of 67 mph was measured at the nearby Alamogordo Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft centered over Arizona put southern New Mexico under a northwest flow, which contained upper waves that destabilized the atmosphere and triggered strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn Spotter estimated winds gusting to 60 mph northeast of La Luz, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft centered over Arizona put southern New Mexico under a northwest flow, which contained upper waves that destabilized the atmosphere and triggered strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn Spotter reported winds estimated at more than 60 mph in Columbus, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft centered over Arizona put southern New Mexico under a northwest flow, which contained upper waves that destabilized the atmosphere and triggered strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measured at 1.4 inches brought street flooding to Columbus, NM. Water was covering the intersection of NM Highways 9 and 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southerly flow was in place over the area. The atmospheric moisture content was almost twice the normal value for early July. The flow was such that thunderstorm movement was slow, subjecting locations to very heavy rainfall for long periods of time. Occasional wet microbursts were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arroyos in the Santa Teresa area became full and led to flooding on NM Highway 273.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over Arizona resulting in northerly flow aloft over southern New Mexico. In addition, a weak back door cold front moved southwestward across the area and stalled near the international border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 58 mph was measured at a mesonet station just west of Silver City, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over Arizona resulting in northerly flow aloft over southern New Mexico. In addition, a weak back door cold front moved southwestward across the area and stalled near the international border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large limbs were blown down in the Mayhill area. Also, 1.25 inches of rain fell within 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over Arizona resulting in northerly flow aloft over southern New Mexico. In addition, a weak back door cold front moved southwestward across the area and stalled near the international border.

A cold front located near the Pecos River and surface based instability along the front resulted in thunderstorm development across southwest Texas.  In addition, abnormally high amounts of atmospheric moisture lead to heavy rainfall for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple power poles were knocked down in Carlsbad, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front located near the Pecos River and surface based instability along the front resulted in thunderstorm development across southwest Texas.  In addition, abnormally high amounts of atmospheric moisture lead to heavy rainfall for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off of a home approximately 15 miles south of Carlsbad, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough embedded within northwesterly flow aloft provided sufficient lift and instability for scattered thunderstorms to develop.

A cold front located near the Pecos River and surface based instability along the front resulted in thunderstorm development across southwest Texas.  In addition, abnormally high amounts of atmospheric moisture lead to heavy rainfall for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One intersection in Jal, NM had 2 to 3 feet of flowing water over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, strong upper low moved into New Mexico from the northwest overnight, with an associated Pacific cold front bringing colder air and lowering snow levels in the Lower Gila Region. Snowfall amounts of 4 or more inches were common north of Silver City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Lower Gila Region. Snowfall accumulations included 4 inches at Lake Roberts and Pinos Altos, and 7 inches on the south slopes of the Pinos Altos range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire developed at a residence along Scenic Road on the west side of Raton, NM around 500 am on February 26th. Sustained winds near 55 mph with wind gusts as high as 69 mph at the Raton Crews Airport and very dry conditions were sufficient to fan the blaze to a nearby home, a carport, and a tree down the street from the initial fire.  The first home and carport were completely destroyed and the second home was partially damaged.

A plume of moist tropical air was streaming northward over New Mexico between an upper high to the east and a Pacific upper trough to the west. In addition, a weak backdoor front was stalled across the area. Disturbances within the southerly flow interacting with the front resulted in thunderstorms that were efficient rain producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baylor Canyon Road and Dripping Springs Road were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over central Texas, which placed southern New Mexico under a moist southeast flow. It was an early start to the North American monsoon in this part of the country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms dropped nearly 3 inches of rain in the Cloudcroft area, which led to flash flooding in and near the town. Water was over two feet deep in parts of Cloudcroft, resulting in some apartments flooded. Rock slides also occurred along Highway 82 in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over central Texas, which placed southern New Mexico under a moist southeast flow. It was an early start to the North American monsoon in this part of the country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred in much of Alamogordo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over central Texas, which placed southern New Mexico under a moist southeast flow. It was an early start to the North American monsoon in this part of the country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph wind gust was measured during a severe thunderstorm at the WSMR golf course. The rainfall total was almost an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms developed along a surface trough across southeast New Mexico during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Due to the slow movement of the storms, localized heavy rainfall occurred over some urbanized areas.
A storm over California moved into the Great Basin and increased the winds at the low and mid levels of the atmosphere. This created some high sustained winds and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 66 mph was also recorded at 135pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough across the Great Basin resulted in southwest flow aloft across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico. The combination of disturbances moving northeast across the area, strong daytime heating and good low level moisture ahead of the dryline resulted in severe weather across the area. The main threat during this episode was large hail and strong damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes and vehicles received significant damage due to the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool pocket of air aloft along with a weak back door front provided a moist, unstable air mass over southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet microburst produced a wind gust of 68 mph at Santa Teresa High School. At least two trees were blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft migrated eastward across central New Mexico during the period, putting southern New Mexico under an easterly mid level flow. A weak inverted trough was embedded in this flow. At the surface, dewpoint depressions were in the 40 to 50 degree F. range, encouraging damaging straight line winds. At the end of the period, the lower levels had moistened up, which facilitated stronger cells and wet microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst with a wind gust measured at 74 mph by the Alamogordo Airport ASOS blew a roof off a trailer and a steeple off a church in nearby Boles Acres. Power lines were also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front stalled over Northeast New Mexico and provided a focus for isolated severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly pea size, but a few stones were the size of a golfball.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front stalled over Northeast New Mexico and provided a focus for isolated severe weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low in northern Chihuahua, Mexico caused winds to be easterly in southern New Mexico. Meanwhile, the flow aloft was southwesterly, resulting in a veering vertical wind profile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn Spotter reported winds gusting to 60 mph in Elephant Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass was in place over Southern New Mexico.
An upper ridge axis was located over the southern plains, putting southern New Mexico under a deep, moist southerly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mescalero Fire Department reported water and mud covering IR-10 at the west end of a large burn scar. Bulldozers were dispatched to clear the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge axis was located over the southern plains, putting southern New Mexico under a deep, moist southerly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Poverty Creek Fire Chief reported that State Highway 59 was flooded in Poverty Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge axis was located over the southern plains, putting southern New Mexico under a deep, moist southerly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms training over far northwestern Sierra County resulted in rainfall amounts of more than 5 inches. The Sierra County Emergency Manager reported a wall of water 12 feet high moved through the Poverty Creek area. One family was evacuated and an unoccupied trailer was washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper disturbance moved slowly southward over southern New Mexico and far west Texas within a moist, unstable air mass. An approaching jet streak from the west along with low level easterly flow provided favorable vertical wind shear and sufficient lift for a severe weather outbreak.

A fast moving, but intense short wave trough moved from northwest to southeast across New Mexico later on the 30th into the 31st. The atmosphere was very unstable during this time and temperatures were well below freezing in many areas. This combination resulted in bands of heavy, dry snow that quickly accumulated over mainly central portions of the state. The hardest hit area was around the Sandia and Manzano Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Though snow amounts were generally 2 inches or less, it was enough to create slick roads.  Luis Segovia-Cabrera, age 39, sustained massive head injuries after he lost control of his SUV and crossed the median on Interstate 40 near exit 96 and crashed into a commercial tractor-trailer head-on. It is suspected he was driving too fast for the conditions and slid on the ice.  Segovia-Cabrera died more than a month later after he was taken off of life support. Three other people sustained injuries from the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall by mid afternoon across portions of western New Mexico. The heavy rainfall fell so quickly that vehicles were swept away into nearby arroyos due to the high water flow. Luckily, no one was injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was washed away in an arroyo near the intersection of Second Canyon Road and Highway N11-49 (also called Navajo Service Route 11).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four feet of water near the intersection of Mountain View Road and Highway 53 made the road impassible.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a vehicle to be washed away in an arroyo.  Law enforcement discovered the vehicle unoccupied with keys in the ignition.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dirt roads were washed away by the heavy rainfall in the Timberlake Ranch subdivision.  Giant boulders blocked the roadways, preventing residents from making it home from the main roadway.

Deep moisture advection across southeast New Mexico, strong upper level lift and substantial deep layer shear resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was knocked down between the cities of Tatum and Lovington, NM.  The exact location of the report was not available.  Therefore, the location was estimated according to radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas ASOS reported a wind gust to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow blanketed much of northern and western New Mexico over a 2 day period in late October. A slow moving storm system dove south across Utah and Arizona before slowing down and moving east across the Land of Enchantment. Precipitation initially began as rain but quickly changed to snow as a strong cold front swiftly moved from northwest to southeast across the state on the 28th. After the front moved though, high temperatures were on average 20 to 30 degrees below normal. As the upper low traversed northern New Mexico, wrap-around precipitation resulted in a second round of snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highest snowfall accumulations were near the Colorado border, including 8 inches at the Raton Port of Entry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving, but intense short wave trough moved from northwest to southeast across New Mexico later on the 30th into the 31st. The atmosphere was very unstable during this time and temperatures were well below freezing in many areas. This combination resulted in bands of heavy, dry snow that quickly accumulated over mainly central portions of the state. The hardest hit area was around the Sandia and Manzano Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow blasted the Sandia and Manzano Mountains with several reports of between 6 and 14 inches of new snow.  Across the east mountains, where around 6 inches of snow was reported, Ken Garrison, a 77-year old man, died after trying to walk home in sub-freezing temperatures after a minor crash disabled his pickup truck. The truck likely slid off the road and was stuck in the snow around Highway 217 and Griego Road around 6 am on the 31st.  His body was found approximately 300 yards from the truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system, and its associated cold front, rapidly moved from west to east across New Mexico during the daytime hours of February 8th.  Once the cold front interacted with Gulf moisture across the eastern plains, a broken line of thunderstorms rapidly developed.  Initially, hail was the main threat with the thunderstorms. However, as the line filled in, it quickly became a thunderstorm high wind event as several bow echoes formed within the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the 63 mph wind gust, sustained winds of up to 53 mph were also measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low moved through the four corners region with a 130kt jet moving into southwest New Mexico. This resulted in heavy snowfall in the higher elevations of the Gila Region and in the Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 9 inches of snow was reported in Cloudcroft by the Fire Department. A COOP Observer reported 5 inches in Mountain Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low moved through the four corners region with a 130kt jet moving into southwest New Mexico. This resulted in heavy snowfall in the higher elevations of the Gila Region and in the Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lookout Mountain SNOTEL site reported 13 inches of snow and the Signal Peak SNOTEL site reported 12 inches of snow. Other reports across the Gila region included 7.7 inches 3 miles west-southwest of Pinos Altos and 6.2 inches 3 miles north-northwest of Silver City from CoCoRaHS observers. Three inches was reported by a trained spotter 3 miles south-southwest of Silver City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across southwestern New Mexico and into far west Texas with cold air already in place at the surface providing a favorable environment for snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum snowfall of 6 inches was reported at San Augustin Pass. Other reports included 3.5 inches 3 miles west southwest of Organ and between 1.5 and 2.5 inches in the Las Cruces area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mountain wave activity resulted in very high winds along the lee side of the Sangre De Cristo Mountains during the morning hours of February 26. Wind gusts topping 60 mph lasted for at least four hours across the Northeast Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained wind speeds between 43 and 47 mph persisted for more than four hours at the Raton Crews Airport. The highest sustained wind speed of 47 mph was reported at both 5 and 7am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mountain wave activity resulted in very high winds along the lee side of the Sangre De Cristo Mountains during the morning hours of February 26. Wind gusts topping 60 mph lasted for at least four hours across the Northeast Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported high sustained winds estimated at 40 mph approximately 6 miles north of Springer at the Texaco Truck Stop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mountain wave activity resulted in very high winds along the lee side of the Sangre De Cristo Mountains during the morning hours of February 26. Wind gusts topping 60 mph lasted for at least four hours across the Northeast Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Crews Airport reported wind gusts over 60 mph between 330am and 8am.  The peak wind gust of 69 mph was recorded at 7am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mountain wave activity resulted in very high winds along the lee side of the Sangre De Cristo Mountains during the morning hours of February 26. Wind gusts topping 60 mph lasted for at least four hours across the Northeast Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported an estimated wind gust of 60 mph 7 miles south of Raton. Winds were predominately out of the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved over New Mexico, and along with a strong cold front, resulted in a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet and snow across southeast New Mexico and west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches of snow accumulated in and near Carlsbad from 11/30/09 to 12/01/09.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved over New Mexico, and along with a strong cold front, resulted in a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet and snow across southeast New Mexico and west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell in and near Tatum from 11/30/09 to 12/01/09 in northern Lea county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low/trough centered over the Colorado Rockies with an associated 150kt jet located over southern New Mexico and far West Texas brought wind gusts over 100 mph to areas along and east of I-25. Considerable damage and power outages were reported in the Sacramento Mountains and east of the Organ Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust of 116 mph was measured 1 mile east-southeast of Organ at San Augustin Pass using a White Sands Missile Range sensor. Another wind gust of 97 mph was measured by a trained spotter 3 miles east-southeast of organ at Aguirre Springs on the east slopes of the Organ Mountains. Numerous power poles were knocked over by the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low/trough centered over the Colorado Rockies with an associated 150kt jet located over southern New Mexico and far West Texas brought wind gusts over 100 mph to areas along and east of I-25. Considerable damage and power outages were reported in the Sacramento Mountains and east of the Organ Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to over 70 mph in the Sacramento Mountains with the strongest gust of 74 mph being reported at the RAWS site 2 miles east-northeast of Mescalero (MSCN5). Several RAWS sites lost power and stopped reporting before the strongest winds, so peak gusts were likely much higher than 74 mph. State of Emergency was declared in and around the Cloudcroft area with widespread power lines down. Electricity not restored for over a week in some locations. Roofs were blown off some houses and an awning/false front fell from a building on Burro Street in Cloudcroft. Dozens of trees were blown over in the area, a few of them falling on houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold airmass encompassed southeast New Mexico and west Texas on 12/03/09.  A shortwave trough translated eastward over the region on the night of the 3rd and resulted in 1 to 3 inches of snow across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow fell in Hope from 12/03/09 to 12/04/09.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front surged through the eastern plains during the evening hours of February 27. Northerly winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts over 60 mph were common for an hour or two behind the cold frontal passage.  Due to dry antecedent conditions, blowing dust reduced visibility below one mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton ASOS reported wind gusts between 62 and 64 mph for 30 minutes.  The peak wind gust of 64 mph occurred at 524pm, as the leading edge of the front went through. Sustained winds between 44 and 51 mph were also recorded during this time frame.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the north shore of Ute Lake, approximately one mile west of Logan, a weather station from Channel 7 in Amarillo reported a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front surged through the eastern plains during the evening hours of February 27. Northerly winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts over 60 mph were common for an hour or two behind the cold frontal passage.  Due to dry antecedent conditions, blowing dust reduced visibility below one mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Cannon AFB gusted to 58 mph between 851 and 854pm. In addition, sustained winds between 40 and 45 mph occurred between 835 and 920pm.  Very similar observations were noted at the Clovis Airport AWOS, approximately three miles north-northwest of Texico. The public also reported sustained winds around 40 mph around 740pm near Grady.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low/trough centered over the Colorado Rockies with an associated 150kt jet located over southern New Mexico and far West Texas brought wind gusts over 100 mph to areas along and east of I-25. Considerable damage and power outages were reported in the Sacramento Mountains and east of the Organ Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust reported was 83 mph at the KE28 AWOS site located 19 miles west-northwest of Holloman Air Force Base. Roof was tore off of the Directorate of Plans Training Mobilization and Security building on the White Sands Missile Range. Two persons were treated for minor injuries from falling debris. Other damage on the White Sands Missile Range included downed power lines, uprooted trees, damage to other buildings, including the police station, broken windows in vehicles and overturned trailers.||Roof were also blown off buildings along with downed power lines in the Chaparral area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief, high winds accompanied a strong upper level storm system and cold front moving across southeast New Mexico.

Snow blanketed much of northern and western New Mexico over a 2 day period in late October. A slow moving storm system dove south across Utah and Arizona before slowing down and moving east across the Land of Enchantment. Precipitation initially began as rain but quickly changed to snow as a strong cold front swiftly moved from northwest to southeast across the state on the 28th. After the front moved though, high temperatures were on average 20 to 30 degrees below normal. As the upper low traversed northern New Mexico, wrap-around precipitation resulted in a second round of snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 5.5 inches of snow was reported around the Gallup area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid level jet max ahead of a developing upper level low pressure system moved quickly northeast over Lincoln County Friday morning. High winds developed at the Sierra Blanca Regional Airport for several hours from late Friday morning into the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High sustained winds 44 mph also at 0955 am. High winds were intermittently recorded through 100 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough across the Great Basin resulted in southwest flow aloft across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico. The combination of disturbances moving northeast across the area, strong daytime heating and good low level moisture ahead of the dryline resulted in severe weather across the area. The main threat during this episode was large hail and strong damaging winds.

A potent storm system brought heavy snow and high winds across the state.  Snowfall started on December 6th across the San Juan Mountains.  Snow increased in coverage and intensity on the 7th, and a final blast of snow came early on the 8th as a cold front swept from west to east across New Mexico.  Many locales on the west facing slopes of the northern mountains were measuring snow in feet rather than inches.  Meanwhile, a mid level jet streak in excess of 80 knots was moving across the southwest mountains northeastward onto the plains.  The strong winds combined with the heavy snow resulted in blizzard conditions across the southwest mountains as well as portions of central New Mexico.  Wind gusts near 135 mph were measured across Magdalena Peak, which set a new record for the highest wind gust measured in the state.   Across the south central mountains and eastern plains, the high winds were a bigger concern than the snow.  Widespread surface wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were measured across this area.  Several buildings, vehicles and trees sustained damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated near 65 mph at the top of Sandia Peak. This high wind event began as a blizzard which began late on December 7th and ended at 11am on the 8th.  The peak wind gust during the blizzard event was 78 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm off the California coast moved inland on the 17th and lifted to the northeast. The mid level winds strengthened as a surface low deepened over southwest Kansas. This produced some strong and gusty winds over central and eastern New Mexico, with the highest winds over Torrance and Lincoln counties. Strong wind gusts around Albuquerque resulted in a highway sign getting blown off its post and struck a car.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a foot of snow fell in blizzard conditions as winds topped out over 100 mph across the peaks of the southwest mountains.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in western Catron County near Luna, but the winds blew hard across the entire area.  The Magdalena Ridge Observatory at 10,600 feet recorded sustained winds between 80 and 120 mph with gusts to 135 mph before the instrument failed.  The wind gusts were literally picking people off their feet. Visibilities were near zero in blowing and drifting snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous gusts over 60 mph were recorded at Sierra Blanca airport.  The peak wind gust of 78 mph was reported at 1035 am.  Between 700 am and 800 am trees were downed from the high winds.  A few trees fell on and damaged two of the ski lifts at Ski Apache and one of the buildings at Ski Apache had roof damage.  The Ski area was closed on the 8th due to the high winds and on the 9th for clean up.  In addition, a 125-foot commercial tower on Buck Mountain was toppled by the high winds, causing radio station and other commercial outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A perturbation within the flow of a long wave upper level trough rotated across New Mexico during the 2nd, 3rd and 4th of December.  A band of moderate snow moved southward across the plains along a mid level cold front where isentropic lift was maximized.  In general, the highest snowfall amounts were reported just east of the central mountain chain in response to easterly upslope flow at the surface, though sporadic higher amounts were noted elsewhere.  The storm resulted in numerous traffic accidents, including a semi-truck that jackknifed along Interstate 40 in Quay County and one death on Interstate 40 at Santa Rosa, when a 1997 Ford Astro van lost control and hit a semi-truck. Jackelyn Zimmerman, 67 years old, was killed in the crash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches were reported, mainly across the western portion of the county.  However, an isolated amount of 8 inches was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought heavy snow and high winds across the state.  Snowfall started on December 6th across the San Juan Mountains.  Snow increased in coverage and intensity on the 7th, and a final blast of snow came early on the 8th as a cold front swept from west to east across New Mexico.  Many locales on the west facing slopes of the northern mountains were measuring snow in feet rather than inches.  Meanwhile, a mid level jet streak in excess of 80 knots was moving across the southwest mountains northeastward onto the plains.  The strong winds combined with the heavy snow resulted in blizzard conditions across the southwest mountains as well as portions of central New Mexico.  Wind gusts near 135 mph were measured across Magdalena Peak, which set a new record for the highest wind gust measured in the state.   Across the south central mountains and eastern plains, the high winds were a bigger concern than the snow.  Widespread surface wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were measured across this area.  Several buildings, vehicles and trees sustained damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow amounts of 2 inches or greater coated the northwest highlands.  The greatest amounts were near Lindrith, where 8 inches of snow was reported.  The heavy snow resulted in one fatality.  Jimmy Shendo, of Jemez Pueblo, was driving a Toyota truck southbound on U.S. 550 in southeast San Juan County, when he lost control and slid into the northbound lanes.  He was struck by an oncoming vehicle at mile marker 110.  Shendo, 59, was not wearing a seatbelt and was partially ejected from the truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system, and its associated cold front, rapidly moved from west to east across New Mexico during the daytime hours of February 8th.  Once the cold front interacted with Gulf moisture across the eastern plains, a broken line of thunderstorms rapidly developed.  Initially, hail was the main threat with the thunderstorms. However, as the line filled in, it quickly became a thunderstorm high wind event as several bow echoes formed within the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of estimated 60 mph winds in downtown Clovis, relayed by Channel 7 in Amarillo, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm over the Pacific Northwest on the 21st moved to the Great Basin on the 22nd, into northern Arizona on the 23rd and crossed New Mexico from northwest to southeast late on the 23rd into the early morning hours of the 24th. Widespread snow fell over northern and central New Mexico, with the highest amounts over the northern, western and south central Mountains as well as a portion of the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2 and 7 inches of snow fell across the Santa Fe Metro Area, with the heaviest amounts falling in the Santa Fe Foothills. Icy roads lead to a three-car crash 7 miles south of El Dorado on Highway 285 on the morning of the 23rd.  Kendall Kalstad, age 59, died when his Volkswagen Beetle was struck head-on by a car that slid on the ice at mile marker 282.  The man's car was then struck from behind by another vehicle that couldn't stop in time.  Three other people were also injured in the accident. The highway was closed for several hours after the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough swinging through the Great Basin continued to bring strong mid level winds over New Mexico. These winds were able to mix down to the surface and create high wind gusts around Las Vegas and Wagon Mound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gusts of 62 mph was also reported in Wagon Mound at 1100 am MST. Wind gusts between 58 mph and 62 mph was recorded at the Las Vegas ASOS between 1127 am and 451 pm MST, while a sustained wind of 44 mph was recorded from 253 pm to 353 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level disturbance traversed east across the northern third of New Mexico on the 7th.  High winds affected areas from the Sacramento Mountains eastward to the east central plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dora Mesonet site recorded numerous five minute observations of high sustained winds. Sustained wind speeds of 40 to 43 mph were recorded between 1000 am and 140 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level disturbance traversed east across the northern third of New Mexico on the 7th.  High winds affected areas from the Sacramento Mountains eastward to the east central plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of high winds at the Sierra Blanca Airport AWOS site. Sustained winds between 40 and 49 mph were recorded between|1055 am and 225 pm MST, while several gusts between 58 and 64 mph were recorded between 1035 am and 335 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid level flow combined with a weak disturbance and associated jet max to produce high winds over portions of central and eastern New Mexico. High sustained winds and wind gusts were reported over a six hour period, between roughly 1100 am and 700 pm MST.

A storm from the Pacific Northwest slowly advanced toward New Mexico on the 25th, reached the Four Corners Region late on the 26th and inched across the state on the 27th. A strong cold front also backed down the eastern plains with strong and gusty winds. Moderate to heavy snow was reported over much of the northern half of the state, with blowing and|drifting snow and poor visibilities over the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow fell across Curry county.

The strong winds combined with the heavy snow resulted in blizzard conditions across the southwest mountains as well as portions of central New Mexico.  Wind gusts near 135 mph were measured across Magdalena Peak, which set a new record for the highest wind gust measured in the state.   Across the south central mountains and eastern plains, the high winds were a bigger concern than the snow.  Widespread surface wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were measured across this area.  Several buildings, vehicles and trees sustained damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 67 mph was recorded at Lake Sumner Village between 120 and 130 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought heavy snow and high winds across the state.  Snowfall started on December 6th across the San Juan Mountains.  Snow increased in coverage and intensity on the 7th, and a final blast of snow came early on the 8th as a cold front swept from west to east across New Mexico.  Many locales on the west facing slopes of the northern mountains were measuring snow in feet rather than inches.  Meanwhile, a mid level jet streak in excess of 80 knots was moving across the southwest mountains northeastward onto the plains.  The strong winds combined with the heavy snow resulted in blizzard conditions across the southwest mountains as well as portions of central New Mexico.  Wind gusts near 135 mph were measured across Magdalena Peak, which set a new record for the highest wind gust measured in the state.   Across the south central mountains and eastern plains, the high winds were a bigger concern than the snow.  Widespread surface wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were measured across this area.  Several buildings, vehicles and trees sustained damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts near 60 mph were estimated across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low moved into northern Mexico allowing cold air to move into southern New Mexico. Northeast upslope flow over the Sacramento Mountains allowed for some areas to receive over 2 feet of snow. Lower totals were reported in the lowlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals reported in the southern Sacramento Mountains included 30 inches in Cloudcroft, 24 inches approximately 9 miles east of Cloudcroft and 9.3 inches in Sunspot.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was reported in Tularosa and up to 5.2 inches 4 miles north of Alamogordo. The city of Alamogordo reported 2 to 4 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow was reported in Kingston. Snow started falling the evening of 11/30/09.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold airmass encompassed southeast New Mexico and west Texas on 12/03/09.  A shortwave trough translated eastward over the region on the night of the 3rd and resulted in 1 to 3 inches of snow across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One half to one inch of snow fell in Jal from 12/03/09 to 12/04/09.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front surged through the eastern plains during the evening hours of February 27. Northerly winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts over 60 mph were common for an hour or two behind the cold frontal passage.  Due to dry antecedent conditions, blowing dust reduced visibility below one mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts of at least 60 mph were reported six miles northwest of Rosebud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved over southeast New Mexico and west Texas late on 12/28/09 and early on 12/29/09.  Cold air over the region combined with this upper storm system to produce a variety of winter weather across southeast New Mexico and west Texas, in addition to snow over the Guadalupe Mountains and southeast New Mexico plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five and one half inches of snow accumulated in Carlsbad by 1115 LST.  Moderate to heavy snowfall in a 2 to 3 hour span resulted in 4 inches of snow accumulation in Carlsbad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved over New Mexico, and along with a strong cold front, resulted in a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet and snow across southeast New Mexico and west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow accumulated at Queen on 11/30/09, but the two day total was 13 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front surged through the eastern plains during the evening hours of February 27. Due to dry antecedent conditions, blowing dust reduced visibility below one mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 mph were estimated at the Northeast New Mexico Detention Facility in Clayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm slowly crossed Northern New Mexico on the 3rd and moved into western Kansas on the 4th. Meanwhile, a surface low deepened rapidly over northeast New Mexico and then lifted into western Kansas. A very tight surface pressure gradient led to widespread reports of high winds and locally damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 69 mph was also recorded at 1245 am MST, and sustained winds of 49 mph were recorded at 1244 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low slowly tracked across northern New Mexico during the 11th and 12th of April. In response, widespread rain and snow showers developed across much of New Mexico. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms also developed and thundersnow lead to significant snowfall amounts from the upper Rio Grande Valley into the southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains as well as in the Sandia Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thundersnow resulted in a quick 6 to 8 inches of snowfall between Pojoaque and Tesuque which prompted Highway 285 to be closed for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds accompanied a strong cold front across western New Mexico during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired NWS employee estimated 50 to 60 mph winds 7 miles northeast of Quemado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moving across the central Rocky Mountains resulted in very strong winds over portions New Mexico over a two day period. On the 22nd, the strongest winds were noted across western New Mexico. However, as the upper level system moved eastward early on the 23rd, the strongest winds shifted to areas along and east of the central mountain chain. Wind gusts up to 70 mph were reported near Las Vegas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired NWS Employee northeast of Quemado estimated wind gusts between 50 and 60mph between 430 and 930pm MST. Estimated sustained winds between 40 and 50mph were also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas ASOS reported a peak wind gust of 70 mph at 330 am MST. However, wind gusts over 60 mph continued through approximately 530 am MST as the winds changed from a southerly to westerly direction due to the passage of a cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm slowly crossed Northern New Mexico on the 3rd and moved into western Kansas on the 4th. Meanwhile, a surface low deepened rapidly over northeast New Mexico and then lifted into western Kansas. A very tight surface pressure gradient led to widespread reports of high winds and locally damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off the abandoned Club Cafe on Historic Route 66 in downtown Santa Rosa. Another roof was blown off a two story home. Several power poles and light posts were damaged or snapped in two. Damage occurred to 87 cars and the buildings at the Northside Auto Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper level trough approached the region from the west resulting in large scale ascent over the Southeast New Mexico Plains.  This, combined with surface convergence along a dryline, and breaks in the low level cloud cover allowed for destabilization of the atmosphere.  Both mid to low level lapse rates were also favorable for strong ascent.  As a Pacific cold front approached the region, convection began to organize resulting in severe storms with damaging winds and large hail.

An unseasonably strong upper level trough moved across central New Mexico.  A strong mid level jet located just down stream of this trough provided enough momentum transfer to the surface, resulting in high winds across the Guadalupe Mountains of New Mexico.

This, combined with surface convergence along a dryline, and breaks in the low level cloud cover allowed for destabilization of the atmosphere. Extensive cloud cover during the day, mostly east of the Pecos River, only resulted in a couple of isolated severe storms over southeast NM for this event.  A mid to upper level shortwave trough and surface convergence a long a lee trough provided the lift during the evening hours.  Moderate surface based instability, 0 to 6 km bulk shear magnitudes near 50 kt and enhanced low level shear due to backing winds across the area resulted in a couple of weak tornadoes being produced by a long lived supercell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported the first brief touchdown of this tornado at 1825 MST about 12 miles southeast of Whites City, NM.  Here, there were 2 tree limbs found within the path of the tornado in addition to golf ball size hail associated with the storm.  At 1842, a trained weather spotter from the local media reported a tornado about 10 miles south southwest of Malaga, NM.  The position of the report was adjusted slightly according to radar.  Please see the attached photos for images of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On April 11, 2009, a surface high northeast of the region resulted in easterly upslope flow over most of the CWA.  Meanwhile, a mid to upper level low near Southern California approached the area from the west.  Although the deepest moisture was confined to the Gulf Coast (due to the previously mentioned surface high) there was enough moisture convergence along a stalled surface boundary and dryline.  Most unstable CAPE values along these features of 1000 j/kg and 0-6 km bulk shear magnitudes of 60 kt allowed for severe storms to develop as the dynamics neared the area during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  A Severe Thunderstorm Watch was issued from 1520-2300 local.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The duration of the hail was estimated for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts during a snow event caused a barn in Aztec to flip over on its roof and land on a fence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts toppled a billboard and ripped the roofs off of four buildings.

Other storms developed along the east slopes of the Sacramento Mountains. These storms fired during the mid afternoon hours and generally moved east-northeast well into the evening. Most of these storms were supercells which dropped large hail across portions of the eastern plains. Rotating wall clouds were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moving southeast out of southern Colorado provided a focus for scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms over central and eastern New Mexico on the afternoon of the 26th. Several storms produced heavy rainfall, gusty winds and penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accumulated to around 4 inches in depth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving south and west across the eastern plains approached the Capitan mountains on May the 20th. Thunderstorms developed ahead of the front producing little rain, but strong winds and numerous lightning strikes. The lightning produced 13 separate fires. The three largest fires burned 44,000 acres of rangeland around Capitan, Arabela and Picacho. At least six ranches received damage by the fires and there was a loss of some livestock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three major fires consumed around 44,000 acres of rangeland from May 20th into the 24th. The three fires were located north of Capitan, around Arabela and near Picacho. The most significant of these was the Fresquez 2 fire north of Capitan. It burned around 10,000 acres north of Highway 246/48 and six ranches suffered fire damage to various degrees. Burned pasture/grassland was the main fire damage as no structures were damaged. However, several cattle were killed in the blaze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary cold front was located over the north central portion of the county warning area this day.  Water vapor imagery indicated the approach of an upper level shortwave trough into the area during the evening hours from the Four Corners Region.  Moderate surface based CAPE values along the front were also noted.  The combination of increased surface convergence along the front, the cooling of the mid to upper levels with the shortwave trough, and increased deep layer shear resulted in one severe storm producing hail over Lea County, NM.

Showers and thunderstorms that developed over the Sangre De Cristo Mountains moved east and organized into a line.  This line of storms surged southeast across the eastern plains bringing another round of damaging winds and small hail to the area.  Ahead of the line, a single supercell developed and moved across northern Quay County.

An embedded shortwave trough within the westerly mid level flow, moved across south central New Mexico.   Combined with moderate surface based instability, one severe storm was able to develop over Eddy County, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary moving southwest through Tucumcari created wind gusts up to 33 kts at the airport.  As the boundary moved southwest across town a local eddy was strong enough to roll a dumpster into a chain link fence at a Tucumcari elementary school and spread the trash across a field. All debris was blown in one direction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small dumpster was rolled over into a chain link fence by a local eddy or dust devil on the edge of an outflow boundary. Debris within the dumpster was spread out across the field of an elementary school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossed New Mexico, creating just enough instability and mid level moisture to trigger some showers and thunderstorms over the state. The low levels of the atmosphere were fairly dry, setting the stage for isolated high downburst winds over the eastern plains.

After a back door cold front ushered in higher moisture all the way to the Arizona border, showers and thunderstorms developed across much of the western and southwest mountains across to the Sacramento Mountains.  Socorro county was one of the hardest hit areas. Slow moving storms dropped over 2 inches of rain in a short amount of time around the San Antonio area.  The Bosque RAWS site reported a total of 2.43 inches of rain.  Slow moving thunderstorms also dropped heavy rains near Milan.  The Grants ASOS reported 1.09 inches of rain. Flash flooding was reported near both of these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 380 was closed between mile markers 2 and 4 east of San Antonio due to water flowing over the roadway after over an estimated 2.5 inches of rain fell in less than 2 hours. Mud and rocks had to be cleared off the road before it was reopened. The flash flooding destroyed an 80 foot section of water supply line just east of the community of San Pedro.  This left residents of San Pedro temporarily without water. In addition, a low water crossing in the Bosquecito area was flooded which halted traffic for a few hours.

Deep moisture remained over the eastern plains on the 21st, allowing thunderstorms to form and produce localized areas of heavy rain. A Mesoscale Convective Complex formed late on the 21st over the east central plains and drifted south during the night. Very heavy rain from slow moving thunderstorms produced significant flash flooding during the early morning of the 22nd around Hagerman.

In addition, one-third of a tin roof was blown off a building and a storage shed was blown 100 yards.

Extensive cloud cover during the day, mostly east of the Pecos River, only resulted in a couple of isolated severe storms over southeast NM for this event.  A mid to upper level shortwave trough and surface convergence a long a lee trough provided the lift during the evening hours.  Moderate surface based instability, 0 to 6 km bulk shear magnitudes near 50 kt and enhanced low level shear due to backing winds across the area resulted in a couple of weak tornadoes being produced by a long lived supercell.

On April 11, 2009, a surface high northeast of the region resulted in easterly upslope flow over most of the CWA.  Meanwhile, a mid to upper level low near Southern California approached the area from the west.  Although the deepest moisture was confined to the Gulf Coast (due to the previously mentioned surface high) there was enough moisture convergence along a stalled surface boundary and dryline.  Most unstable CAPE values along these features of 1000 j/kg and 0-6 km bulk shear magnitudes of 60 kt allowed for severe storms to develop as the dynamics neared the area during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  A Severe Thunderstorm Watch was issued from 1520-2300 local.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The duration of the hail was estimated for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through southeast New Mexico on this day, resulting in a tight surface pressure gradient across the region.  The northeast surface winds behind this front briefly gusted to 60 mph across the Guadalupe Mountains of New Mexico.
Unseasonably high moisture content through the atmospheric column, an upper level jet max and a weak mid-level shortwave trough combined resulting in heavy rainfall across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported multiple county roads were barricaded near Malaga, NM due to high water.  Sandbags were also used to prevent flooding near homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably high moisture content through the atmospheric column, an upper level jet max and a weak mid-level shortwave trough combined resulting in heavy rainfall across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sheriff's office reported that they were barricading low water crossing near Otis due to 1 to 2 feet of flowing water through some intersections.  Sandbags were also used to prevent flooding near homes.  No damage was reported, however.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather erupted across portions of the eastern plains during the afternoon hours as a strong shortwave moved across northern New Mexico.  Dewpoints in the mid 50s to low 60s, abundant instability, and 40 to 50 knots of 0 to 6 km shear was present across the northeast quarter of the state.  Storms formed along the east slopes of the central mountain chain and quickly became supercellular as they moved into more unstable air.  Very large hail, damaging wind gusts and an isolated tornado resulted from the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough across the Great Basin resulted in southwest flow aloft across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico. The combination of disturbances moving northeast across the area, strong daytime heating and good low level moisture ahead of the dryline resulted in severe weather across the area. The main threat during this episode was large hail and strong damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cotton and alfalfa crops were damaged near Atoka, NM.  The Quentin Rogers cotton fields were stripped down to bare ground, while a 3 foot tall hay field near East Blevins Road and Highway 385 was stripped down to 10 inches tall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system and associated cold front resulted in isolated convection across southeast New Mexico.  One severe thunderstorm with small hail and damaging winds dove south across Lea County New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two distribution power poles were blown down due to damaging winds.  A 60 mph wind gust was also measured at the Lea County Coop Plant.

Light showers formed behind a strong gust front during the evening hours across southwest Quay County. Ironically, the outflow boundary from the light showers proved to be stronger than the main gust front they formed behind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SchoolNet site in House reported a 63 mph wind gust from the south-southwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms moved up in Quay County from Roosevelt and Curry counties during the afternoon hours.  A particularly strong storm embedded within the activity sent out a strong outflow boundary across eastern portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gusts was reported at the SchoolNet site at Frost Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary dropped south into the Northeast Plains providing plenty of low level, up slope flow, while a weak northwest flow aloft created decent shear. This created a few long lived thunderstorms, one of which produced large hail and highi wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several customers at the Texaco truck stop and a county sheriff estimated wind gusts around 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail between 0.88 inches and 1.75 inches was reported between 415pm and 430pm MST in Clovis. Some roof damage was reported, with leaves stripped off trees and damage to corn and tomato crops.

Severe weather erupted across portions of the eastern plains during the afternoon hours as a strong shortwave moved across northern New Mexico.  Dewpoints in the mid 50s to low 60s, abundant instability, and 40 to 50 knots of 0 to 6 km shear was present across the northeast quarter of the state.  Storms formed along the east slopes of the central mountain chain and quickly became supercellular as they moved into more unstable air.  Very large hail, damaging wind gusts and an isolated tornado resulted from the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public lost a truck window at the intersection of State Highways 392 and 469.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle shortwave in northwest flow aloft helped to spark thunderstorms across western New Mexico.  As storms moved east into better moisture, they became severe and produced large hail and damaging winds. Areas in and southeast of Roswell were the hardest hit and sustained significant damage from the powerful straight line winds.  The Roswell Industrial Airport was on the southwest edge of the strongest winds.

A cold front located near the Pecos River and surface based instability along the front resulted in thunderstorm development across southwest Texas.  In addition, abnormally high amounts of atmospheric moisture lead to heavy rainfall for some locations.
High pressure aloft centered over Arizona put southern New Mexico under a northwest flow, which contained upper waves that destabilized the atmosphere and triggered strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Employees at the Deming Aerostat Site reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms that developed over the Sangre De Cristo Mountains moved east and organized into a line.  This line of storms surged southeast across the eastern plains bringing another round of damaging winds and small hail to the area.  Ahead of the line, a single supercell developed and moved across northern Quay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Schoolnet site reported high winds between 549 and 553 pm MST at Ute Lake. A peak wind gust of 60 mph was reported at 553 pm with sustained winds over 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained over the eastern plains on the 21st, allowing thunderstorms to form and produce localized areas of heavy rain. A Mesoscale Convective Complex formed late on the 21st over the east central plains and drifted south during the night. Very heavy rain from slow moving thunderstorms produced significant flash flooding during the early morning of the 22nd around Hagerman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rio Felix was flowing at a depth of ten feet and began to flood the area around Cherokee Road, three miles west of Hagerman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, slow moving thunderstorms dropped copious amounts of rainfall over northern Union County. Around 2 inches of rain fell during the afternoon of the 26th. The previous day between 3.5 and 5.5 inches of rain fell over the same area.  The area was primed for rapid runoff to occur with additional heavy rainfall. The heavy rains produced good rises in the normally dry Cimarron River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to 10 inches of water was running over State Highway 456 between mile markers 16 and 30.  Cars were submerged in the water and mud and had to be pulled out near mile marker 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate northwest flow over the eastern plains and a deep layer of moisture combined to produce widespread severe weather over the eastern plains. In addition, high winds toppled a large tree in Albuquerque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were snapped off of trees.
