Northwest flow developed over New Mexico on the 3rd as a shortwave ridge tightened over the southwest. Isolated severe thunderstorms developed over the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and moved southeast onto the east central plains by late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported along Interstate 40 at Exit 267.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow developed over New Mexico on the 3rd as a shortwave ridge tightened over the southwest. Isolated severe thunderstorms developed over the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and moved southeast onto the east central plains by late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail damaged a lot of vegetation and piled up three inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow developed over New Mexico on the 3rd as a shortwave ridge tightened over the southwest. Isolated severe thunderstorms developed over the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and moved southeast onto the east central plains by late afternoon.
A fresh supply of moisture behind a back door cold front, along with good directional shear, produced large hail and high winds over the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds between 700 and 730 pm MST ripped telephone polls in half and broke tree branches 8 to 12 inches in diameter. Electrical power was out for about 7 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow across the eastern plains and a dry surface allowed for gusty downburst winds to develop across portions of the eastern plains.

A back door cold front early on the 17th provided a fresh supply of low level moisture over eastern New Mexico. Southeast low level winds and moderate northwest flow aloft allowed for strong shear, producing long lived and severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two foot diameter, 60 foot tall tree was blown down in the front of a police station. Winds were estimated at 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front early on the 17th provided a fresh supply of low level moisture over eastern New Mexico. Southeast low level winds and moderate northwest flow aloft allowed for strong shear, producing long lived and severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were large tree branches and power lines downed from high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front early on the 17th provided a fresh supply of low level moisture over eastern New Mexico. Southeast low level winds and moderate northwest flow aloft allowed for strong shear, producing long lived and severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a wind gust to 68 mph near Highway 206.  Blowing dust reduced the visibility to near zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front early on the 17th provided a fresh supply of low level moisture over eastern New Mexico. Southeast low level winds and moderate northwest flow aloft allowed for strong shear, producing long lived and severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high wind gust estimated at 60 mph reduced the visibility to a quarter of a mile in blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fresh supply of moisture behind a back door cold front, along with good directional shear, produced large hail and high winds over the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 60 mph was recorded at the SchoolNet site in House.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southerly flow was in place over the area. The atmospheric moisture content was almost twice the normal value for early July. The flow was such that thunderstorm movement was slow, subjecting locations to very heavy rainfall for long periods of time. Occasional wet microbursts were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico Highway 27 was washed south of Hillsboro, NM. It affected a 10 foot stretch of the road, covering a half lane with a 6 foot drop off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the base of a vigorous trough moved across the state, high winds mixed down to the surface by early afternoon across portions of western New Mexico as well as just east of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.   These high wind gusts continued through the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas ASOS reported a wind gust to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued deep moisture and good shear over the eastern half of New Mexico resulted in another round of severe thunderstorms over primarily the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters and law enforcement reported golfball size hail in Los Alamos for at least 5 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front squeezed through the central mountain passes and canyons into the Rio Grande Valley early in the morning, increasing low level moisture and setting up a surface boundary nearby. Thunderstorms formed south of Albuquerque in the afternoon and moved north across eastern portions of the Duke City. Three quarters to over two inches of rain fell over the eastern heights and foothills in less than one hours time. This resulted in major street flooding with up to two and a half feet of standing water on streets, stranding vehicles.  Other flooding around Abiquiu washed out a county road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was two and one half feet of standing water at the intersection of Juan Tabo and Candelaria with cars stranded in the water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on County Road 155, washing out culverts and parts of the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water overflowed Walnut Creek about 2 miles south of Lake Arthur, flooding a portion of State Road 2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty Air Force Base employee reported tree branches three to four inches in diameter were snapped off trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds associated with a cold front moved across southeast New Mexico on this day.

A complex of severe storms first developed over eastern NM on this day, and then moved south into southeast NM.  This complex of storms formed on the eastern periphery of an upper level ridge west of the region, with support from a mid level shortwave trough.  A similar scenario was also observed the following day.

A similar scenario was also observed the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was no significant damage reported with this estimated gust.

Monsoonal moisture was responsible for a thunderstorm with heavy rain west of Santa Fe, which resulted in water flowing out of an arroyo at a height of two feet.  Lightning from a thunderstorm in Rio Rancho struck and killed a 26 year old man. Six other members of his family sustained injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jarrod Truesdale, a 26 year old man, was killed by a lightning strike in the parking lot of Rio Rancho High School before a fireworks show.  His wife, 26, was also struck and sustained serious injuries.  Their 4 kids, ranging from 18 months to 7 years in age, as well as the wife's 21 year old sister also sustained injuries.  The family was headed for their vehicle to seek shelter from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with plenty of low level moisture to generate showers and thunderstorms over central New Mexico. Four people in a diversion channel were swept away by fast moving water, resulting in one death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four people in the North Diversion Channel under Interstate 25 at Montgomery Blvd. were swept downstream by swift moving water from a nearby thunderstorm that was producing heavy rain. Three men were able to pull themselves out of the Channel, but a fourth male, Brandon Clifford Jordan, was found dead the next day in the Channel at 2nd street and Alameda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture was responsible for a thunderstorm with heavy rain west of Santa Fe, which resulted in water flowing out of an arroyo at a height of two feet.  Lightning from a thunderstorm in Rio Rancho struck and killed a 26 year old man. Six other members of his family sustained injuries.
A storm system moved into western New Mexico creating strong instability. Plenty of moisture was also present to help set off scattered showers and thunderstorms over western New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home was damaged from excess runoff from the Rio San Jose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike from a thunderstorm over northwest New Mexico damaged the radio transmitter on Rabbit Peak southwest of Chama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over northwest New Mexico during the early morning hours produced a cloud to ground lightning strike that hit the 96.1 FM KZRM Radio transmitter on Rabbit Peak, just west northwest of Chama. The station was off the air for at least three weeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper disturbance moved slowly southward over southern New Mexico and far west Texas within a moist, unstable air mass. An approaching jet streak from the west along with low level easterly flow provided favorable vertical wind shear and sufficient lift for a severe weather outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 2.5 inches of rain resulted in flash flooding around Palomas. At least 5 homes were inundated and several cars were stranded in flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system moved across southern California and Nevada, into Arizona and eventually New Mexico. The system tapped into abundant moisture from the south that overspread the state, setting the stage for significant rain over much of northern and central New Mexico. The higher elevations of the central and northern mountains received a foot or more of snow.

A potent storm system moved across southern California and Nevada, into Arizona and eventually New Mexico. The system tapped into abundant moisture from the south that overspread the state, setting the stage for significant rain over much of northern and central New Mexico. The higher elevations of the central and northern mountains received a foot or more of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five telephone poles were snapped along Highway 70, about 4 miles west of Roswell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold upper low moved across the southern Rockies producing heavy snow in the Sacramento Mountains. In addition, strong winds aloft mixed down to the surface resulting in wind gusts to greater than 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative Observer reported 9 inches of snow on the ground from an overnight snowstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds topped 60 mph across localized areas of the south central mountains very late on the 27th.  These strong winds preceded a strong cold front that blasted through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Sierra Blanca Regional Airport reached a peak wind gust of 67 mph at 1035 pm MST.  However, wind gusts did exceed 60 mph between 955 and 1055 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds topped 60 mph across localized areas of the south central mountains very late on the 27th.  These strong winds preceded a strong cold front that blasted through the area.
Strong winds aloft combined with a surface low pressure system in northeast New Mexico resulted in strong winds across the central highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS reported a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell much of the day on the 25th, but heavier accumulations did not start until late in the evening as upslope flow interacted with weak disturbances in northwest flow aloft.  The greatest snowfall amounts were generally above 8000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were common near Angel Fire and Eagle Nest.  The highest total of 12 inches was reported 12 miles south-southeast of Angel Fire at an elevation of 10080 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system tracked from the Four Corners area east-southeast across New Mexico during the weekend of the 14th and 15th.   As it exited the area, a strong back door cold front surged south across the eastern plains.  Heaviest snow amounts were generally above 8000 feet across north central and northwest New Mexico.  However, modest accumulations were also noted across the northeast behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranging from 8 to 10 inches were reported by the Hopewell SNOTEL as well as members of the public across the higher elevations of the San Juan Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: KVII-TV in Amarillo relayed a view report of penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cloud to ground lightning from a thunderstorm between Farmington and Bloomfield struck a 24 year old man while he was on his horse, riding along a metal fence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 year old man was critically injured when he was struck by lightning while riding his horse along a metal fence. The horse was killed. The man's hat had a four inch hole in it from the lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow blanketed much of northern and western New Mexico over a 2 day period in late October. A slow moving storm system dove south across Utah and Arizona before slowing down and moving east across the Land of Enchantment. Precipitation initially began as rain but quickly changed to snow as a strong cold front swiftly moved from northwest to southeast across the state on the 28th. After the front moved though, high temperatures were on average 20 to 30 degrees below normal. As the upper low traversed northern New Mexico, wrap-around precipitation resulted in a second round of snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highest snow accumulations were found near the Colorado border just north of La Plata and Cedar Hill.  Up to 10.5 inches of snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to previous days, storms formed over the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the afternoon.  However, unlike the days previous, one thunderstorm was able to get organized and subsequently dropped severe hail over Mora County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail lasted 10 minutes and covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm over northwest Catron County produced cloud to ground lightning. One of the lightning strikes struck a man who was standing under a 30 foot tall tree to get out of the rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harry Beggerly, 61, died near Quemado Lake from a lightning strike on the afternoon of August 30, while seeking shelter from the rain under a 30 foot tall tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system brought snow to much of New Mexico over a four day period. Snow began over the northern mountains on November 28th and lingered through the morning hours of the 30th. Then, as the system crossed northern old Mexico, significant snows developed over the south central mountains and adjacent plains to the east from November 30th through December 1st. More than one foot of snow was reported across several of these locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snow accumulations blanketed eastern Lincoln County.  The heaviest amounts were across southeast Lincoln County where up to 9 inches of snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front dropping south across the eastern plains combined with sufficient low level moisture to trigger isolated severe thunderstorms over east central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail and strong winds knocked siding off of a home, broke house windows and piled up over one foot deep on Route 202, east of U.S. Highway 70. A funnel cloud was also reported to be in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate northwest flow over the eastern plains and a deep layer of moisture combined to produce widespread severe weather over the eastern plains. In addition, high winds toppled a large tree in Albuquerque.

Severe weather erupted across portions of the eastern plains during the afternoon hours as a strong shortwave moved across northern New Mexico.  Dewpoints in the mid 50s to low 60s, abundant instability, and 40 to 50 knots of 0 to 6 km shear was present across the northeast quarter of the state.  Storms formed along the east slopes of the central mountain chain and quickly became supercellular as they moved into more unstable air.  Very large hail, damaging wind gusts and an isolated tornado resulted from the storms.

An upper level storm system, and its associated cold front, rapidly moved from west to east across New Mexico during the daytime hours of February 8th.  Once the cold front interacted with Gulf moisture across the eastern plains, a broken line of thunderstorms rapidly developed.  Initially, hail was the main threat with the thunderstorms. However, as the line filled in, it quickly became a thunderstorm high wind event as several bow echoes formed within the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report relayed by Channel 7 in Amarillo, TX.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough across the Great Basin resulted in southwest flow aloft across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico. The combination of disturbances moving northeast across the area, strong daytime heating and good low level moisture ahead of the dryline resulted in severe weather across the area. The main threat during this episode was large hail and strong damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The most extensive damage during this event was confined to the eastern portions Jal, NM with the most notable damage along State Highway 128 in the vicinity of the Lea County/Jal Airport.  At this location, a small single engine plane was flipped over.   Also, numerous aircraft hanger doors were blown in due to the high winds which resulted in extensive damage to several aircraft storage buildings.  Along State Highway 128, 28 power poles stretched along the southern edge of the highway were blown over into the eastbound lane of traffic.  Overall, a total of 60 power poles were damaged.  Closer to the city, numerous residential structures received significant roof damage, mainly in the form of lost roof shingles.  Several trees were also downed near the Jal Lake Recreational Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front stalled over Northeast New Mexico and provided a focus for isolated severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly golf ball size, however, a few stones were around two inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced strong outflow boundaries over the east central plains that resulted in isolated strong wind gusts.

A cold front located near the Pecos River and surface based instability along the front resulted in thunderstorm development across southwest Texas.  In addition, abnormally high amounts of atmospheric moisture lead to heavy rainfall for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter's car windshield was broken and the hail also dented the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Though no hail or heavy rain occurred around the area, a brief tornado formed south of Tres Piedras causing damage to a barn and fencing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado, likely a landspout, occurred two miles south of the Highway 64 and 285 intersection.  The tornado tore the roof off a barn and ripped up some fencing.
